header.title: Daily FI Market Intelligence: 2026-06-30 NY Close
header.subtitle: Real yields cheapened while risk assets absorbed the move.
header.stance: Rates sold off; credit and EM stayed selective.
header.as_of_note: NY close 2026-06-30; labor, supply, dollar and primary-market absorption set the next read.

overall_summary.headline: Real yields led the selloff while equities kept risk appetite intact.
overall_summary.summary: Treasury yields rose across the curve after firm labor-demand data, with the 10Y real yield carrying more of the move than breakevens. Equities and volatility accepted higher rates, while listed credit, DXY and EM debt kept the fixed-income read selective.
bullets: 10Y TIPS +4 bp to 2.20%; BE10Y +2 bp to 2.24%.
May JOLTS openings held near 7.6 million; confidence rose to 91.2.
overall_summary.ny_close_causal_narrative: Firm openings kept the Fed path restrictive enough to lift real yields, while lower oil restrained breakevens and equities absorbed the rate move. Credit and EM gauges lagged, leaving payrolls, supply and dollar liquidity as the confirmation path.

watch_events.watch_line: Hiring decides whether the JOLTS selloff extends.
watch_events.market_trigger: ADP and payrolls above the expected 100k-118k range with firm wages.
watch_events.market_implication: Firm hiring keeps 2Y, 5Y and 10Y real yields exposed to another repricing.
watch_events.confirms_if: Hiring cools and wage pressure does not offset the slowdown.
watch_events.challenges_if: Payrolls surprise higher with stable participation or stronger earnings.
watch_events.sensitive_channel: UST front end, 10Y real yield and 2s10s curve
watch_events.why_it_matters: Openings stayed firm; hiring decides whether the Fed-path read becomes durable.

watch_events.title: Treasury Bills And July Coupon Schedule
watch_events.watch_line: Auction demand decides whether supply adds to real-yield pressure.
watch_events.market_trigger: Weak bill or coupon demand, wider concessions, or persistent funding pressure.

watch_events.confirms_if: Bills and coupons clear with stable demand and contained concessions.
watch_events.challenges_if: Demand weakens while labor data keeps yields elevated.
watch_events.sensitive_channel: Bills, front-end funding, 10Y yield and 30Y yield
watch_events.why_it_matters: The June 30 selloff needs absorption evidence before supply becomes a driver.

watch_events.market_implication: Higher oil lifts breakevens; a firmer dollar tightens EM and hard-currency debt.
watch_events.confirms_if: Oil stays contained while DXY eases with softer labor data.
watch_events.challenges_if: Crude rebounds or DXY extends the monthly gain.

watch_events.why_it_matters: The close was real-yield led because oil restrained inflation compensation.

watch_events.title: Primary-Market Absorption

watch_events.market_implication: Weak concessions would keep credit behind the equity rally despite open access.

watch_events.challenges_if: Weaker borrowers delay deals or face sharply higher coupons.

watch_events.title: Japan, ECB And EM Dollar Liquidity
watch_events.watch_line: Japan long-end pressure, ECB inflation language and DXY decide global spillover.

watch_events.market_implication: Global duration stress would limit U.S. long-end recovery and tighten EM access.
watch_events.confirms_if: JGB demand stabilizes and ECB rhetoric softens with oil contained.
watch_events.challenges_if: Japan 30Y yields rise again or EM spreads widen with DXY strength.

long_thesis.english_markdown:
# Daily FI Market Intelligence - 2026-06-30 NY Close

## Opening Fixed-Income Thesis

June 30 closed with U.S. rates cheapening, risk assets still able to carry the move, and global fixed income left with a cleaner labor-data test than it had at the start of the session. The main Treasury curve rose across the maturities that matter for the close: 2Y finished at 4.14%, 10Y at 4.44%, and 30Y at 4.91%. The move was large enough to interrupt the prior week's duration relief, yet it came with the S&P 500 up 0.79%, the Nasdaq 100 up 1.68%, the semiconductor index up 3.92%, and VIX down to 16.45. That pairing matters because it says higher yields were accepted by risk markets, not rejected through a broad de-risking impulse.

The rates mechanism was direct. May JOLTS openings held near 7.6 million, above the softer number the front end wanted, while June consumer confidence edged up to 91.2 even as the labor-perception details softened. The 10Y real yield rose 4 bp to 2.20%, more than the 2 bp increase in the 10Y breakeven to 2.24%. Oil helped keep the inflation-compensation channel contained, with WTI down to 70.10 and almost 20% lower on the month. The close therefore leaned on real yields and Fed-path caution more than on a pure energy-inflation scare. The curve steepened only modestly, leaving the bigger question in the front end and belly while fiscal long-end pressure stayed secondary for this close.

Credit and funding gave a more selective answer than equities. Japanese banks and corporates were still able to raise foreign-currency debt at scale, with Mizuho and SMFG pricing almost 10 billion dollars and Japanese overseas corporate issuance reaching about 54 billion dollars for the quarter. That is real primary-market absorption. At the same time, LQD fell 0.57%, EMB fell 0.39%, and weaker-borrower stories stayed alive through China's effort to curb higher-yield offshore municipal-linked issuance, Dish's bankruptcy process, and renewed interest in stressed private credit. Credit closed with functioning primary access and weaker listed gauges, so the equity rally lacked broad spread-like confirmation.

The global backdrop kept the U.S. close from becoming a one-market story. Japan's super-long yields rose as fiscal plans strained the long end, even as Meiji Yasuda signaled more than 2 trillion yen of super-long JGB demand. ECB officials at Sintra treated lower oil as helpful but kept second-round inflation effects in view; French and German inflation cooled, yet the policy reaction function stayed conditional. EM carried the same split: record first-half international bond sales and inflows into Indonesia and India showed open access, while South Africa, Colombia, Venezuela, Kenya, and China all showed why dollar liquidity and country balance sheets still matter. The next read comes from whether ADP, payrolls, Treasury supply, DXY, oil, and primary-market pricing validate the real-yield selloff or let duration recover.

## UST Rates, Fed Pricing, Real Yields, And Curve Structure

Treasuries cheapened because the data mix denied the front end a clean labor-cooling signal. The 2Y rose 4 bp to 4.14%, the 5Y rose 5 bp to 4.19%, the 10Y rose 6 bp to 4.44%, and the 30Y rose 5 bp to 4.91%. The move was broad but not disorderly. The curve steepened a little from a still-flat base, with 2s10s widening 2 bp to 30 bp and 2s30s widening 1 bp to 77 bp. The 2s5s10s butterfly stayed at -20 bp, and the 2s10s30s butterfly moved 3 bp to -17 bp, keeping the focus on policy and real-yield repricing over a single long-end supply break.

The macro trigger was May JOLTS. Openings came in at 7.594 million, roughly steady from the prior month and firmer than a consensus near 7.3 million. Hires around 5.2 million kept the labor-demand story from rolling over before the rest of the week's employment data. Consumer confidence added a second, more mixed input. The Conference Board index rose to 91.2 from a revised 90.6, helped by lower gasoline prices, while labor-market perceptions inside the survey softened. Rates traded the combination as resilient enough to keep the Fed cautious, with the softer internals left for payrolls and wage detail before easing confidence could build.

Real yields carried the heavier part of the move. The 10Y TIPS real yield rose 4 bp to 2.20%, while the 10Y breakeven rose 2 bp to 2.24%. That split is important for the close because the nominal selloff did not require a renewed oil shock. WTI ended at 70.10, down 0.92% on the day and sharply lower over the month, and Brent was around 73.46. Lower energy prices helped restrain inflation compensation, but firm openings lifted the real policy-rate hurdle. The result was a higher-rate close with inflation relief still present in the background.

The curve says the same thing in a different form. A 2 bp steepening in 2s10s shows the long end participated, though the move was small compared with the month-to-date flattening still embedded in the curve. The 2Y remained 16 bp higher on the month, while the 10Y was roughly flat on the month and the 30Y was lower. That month profile says the June close is still living with a front-end policy premium and a long-end market that had already received real-yield and breakeven relief earlier in the month. June 30 reversed part of that relief without erasing the broader month-end improvement.



HSBC's dollar warning helps connect the Fed path, the currency market, and the curve. The bank warned that a further tightening signal could make the dollar rally more forceful, and it also pointed to the risk that the early-year steepener consensus had become vulnerable. That is consistent with the June 30 tape: front-end and intermediate yields rose, the dollar firmed, and the curve steepened only slightly after a month in which the front end had stayed the more restrictive part of the market. The currency call did not cause the Treasury move. It captured the same macro configuration visible across rates and FX: resilient U.S. data, limited Fed guidance, and a market still prepared to charge more real yield before payrolls.

Treasury supply and auction calendar items stay on the watch list for this session. Late-June bill results, settlement dates, and early-July note and bond reopening schedules matter for absorption, but the close did not provide enough direct auction-demand evidence to make supply the lead cause of the yield move. The useful rates conclusion is narrower: firm labor demand lifted real yields and kept the front end exposed, while oil relief kept breakevens from taking full control. ADP and payrolls now decide whether the selloff extends through the belly or fades back into the prior duration bid.

## Credit, Funding, And Primary-Market Absorption

Credit conditions were open but selective. The best primary-market evidence came from Japan's overseas funding channel. Mizuho Financial Group and Sumitomo Mitsui Financial Group priced almost 10 billion dollars of U.S.-currency debt at the start of the week, helping push Japanese corporate foreign-currency issuance to about 54 billion dollars for the quarter. That quarterly total was more than 80% above the year-earlier period and the first time Japanese company foreign bond sales exceeded 50 billion dollars in a quarter in the available history. Panasonic also moved into the dollar market, extending the point that high-quality issuers could still use overseas depth to finance expansion.

The funding relevance is the cost of global capital. Japanese firms are borrowing abroad while local rates are higher and the yen is historically weak. A weaker yen increases the local-currency proceeds once dollar or euro borrowings are swapped back, while higher domestic rates reduce the old advantage of local funding. That combination creates issuance volume without proving that every borrower has cheap access. It is a sign of market depth for strong names and banks, and a reminder that all-in costs, currency hedges, and maturity choices still decide who can fund.

China gave the other side of the market-access story. Authorities discouraged selected firms from issuing higher-yield offshore debt, a step aimed at municipal borrowers that already carry more than 100 billion dollars of overseas bonds. The message is not a U.S. credit-spread driver for the June 30 close, but it is an important funding-quality signal. Policy makers are trying to prevent weaker local-government-linked borrowers from locking in expensive external debt. That curbs supply for risky names, but it also underlines refinancing pressure when domestic support, offshore access, and investor tolerance are all policy-sensitive.

Stressed corporate and private-credit stories kept the quality divide visible. Dish entered bankruptcy to help manage debt repayment, with court filings listing assets between 1 billion and 10 billion dollars and liabilities between 10 billion and 50 billion dollars. In private credit, Len Tannenbaum's planned return to the business near a described crisis shows distress capital preparing for opportunities while stress remains visible. AI-related debt also stayed on the watch list after financial-stability officials raised concern about maturity mismatch between long-lived physical assets and the debt used to fund them.

The market gauges fit a selective interpretation. LQD fell 0.57%, HYG slipped 0.05%, and JNK was down 0.03%. Those moves combine rates, duration, and spread inputs, so they should be treated as risk gauges, not cash-spread proof. Still, they contrast with the strong equity tape and show that credit did not close with the same enthusiasm as semiconductors or the Nasdaq. The primary window was open for strong borrowers, but listed credit and stressed-borrower news left the read closer to differentiated access than broad credit easing.



AI funding sits between credit and cross-asset because it is both a growth story and a financing story. The equity market rewarded semiconductors and AI-linked growth on June 30, but the debt side is more complicated. Large data-center and chip investment programs require long-lived physical assets, grid connections, construction labor, and financing tenors that may not match cash-flow timing. That is why concern about AI leverage matters for credit even when the stock market is rising. Capital intensity, wage pressure in skilled trades, and maturity mismatch can make the funding cycle more sensitive to real yields than the equity tape implies.

Market plumbing added a smaller but useful note. Erroneous trades forced DTCC into a rare double-settlement day. The issue was described as an operational snag, and DTCC's fixed-income and equity settlement footprint is large enough to matter for timing, collateral movement, and dealer balance-sheet attention. That item does not explain the UST selloff. Operational friction can amplify quarter-end or issuance-day strain if it coincides with heavy settlement, bill supply, or risk transfer, so it remains a funding watch item.

## Global Duration, Central Banks, And Fiscal Transmission

Global duration remained a separate source of pressure. Japan was the clearest example. Super-long JGB yields rose as Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's spending roadmap increased concern about fiscal expansion and long-end supply. The 30Y yield climbed 11 bp to 3.94%, and the 20Y yield rose 9 bp to 3.64%. That is a developed-market duration event with a different source than U.S. JOLTS: fiscal credibility, long-end demand, and the ability of domestic liability buyers to absorb supply.

Domestic demand still mattered in Japan. The two-year JGB auction drew stronger demand than the 12-month average, and the two-year yield fell after the result. Meiji Yasuda also planned to lift super-long JGB purchases to more than 2 trillion yen in the fiscal year ending March 2027, doubling its earlier plan after yields moved to levels it viewed as more attractive. That gives Japan a two-sided duration story. Fiscal risk cheapened the long end, while insurance demand and front-end caution kept parts of the curve anchored. For U.S. Treasuries, the read-through is global term-premium sensitivity, with no direct same-day cash Treasury demand signal needed.

Europe's story was more about energy pass-through and central-bank patience. French inflation slowed to 2.0% in June from 2.8%, and German inflation cooled to 2.4% from 2.7%. Lower oil helped both prints and reduced immediate pressure on the ECB. Yet the Sintra communication did not declare the shock finished. Philip Lane said second-round effects from energy prices can take time to emerge. Olaf Sleijpen said the full extent of the inflation shock was still unclear. Pierre Wunsch said the case for another hike was weaker, while Olli Rehn described the Middle East shock as stagflationary in nature. Primoz Dolenc left room for a July pause if energy prices stay near current levels and second-round effects do not appear.

That mix gives global duration partial easing of pressure while policy risk remains active. Lower oil and softer inflation prints help Bunds and euro-area duration, but the ECB remains sensitive to food, services, wages, and inflation expectations. The policy risk is no longer a straight energy spike, yet it is still a reaction-function problem. As long as central banks describe the shock as data-dependent and lagged, global duration cannot treat oil relief as a full policy pivot.

Australia and New Zealand added the same lesson from different angles. The RBA held its cash rate at 4.35% and used the pause to assess how prior tightening was flowing through housing demand and broader conditions. Minutes still described policy as restrictive and warned that fuel disruptions could lift underlying inflation. The IMF said New Zealand should raise rates toward neutral this year to keep expectations anchored and avoid a sharper response later. These were not June 30 UST price drivers. They reinforce the global point that central banks are using lower oil and softer growth signals to buy time, while inflation expectations keep the terminal-rate debate alive.



The euro-area inflation details also change how U.S. duration reads lower oil. France slowing to 2.0% and Germany slowing to 2.4% make the energy channel disinflationary at the margin, yet Spain's earlier strength and the ECB's focus on food and services keep the policy path from moving mechanically with crude. Lane's point about lagged second-round effects, Sleijpen's uncertainty language, and Rehn's stagflation framing all push the same way: oil relief lowers one immediate input, while central banks still need proof that wages, services, and expectations will follow. That keeps global real-rate pricing alive even when headline inflation improves.

Central Europe added a cleaner easing contrast. Poland's inflation slowed to 2.5% from 3.1%, below the prior month and below the market forecast. That reduced local rate-hike risk and showed how quickly an energy-driven shock can fade when domestic price pressure cooperates. The contrast with South Africa, Colombia, and Japan is useful: some markets receive lower-oil relief as a path toward easier policy, while others face inflation expectations, fiscal pressure, or currency constraints. U.S. long-end pricing is influenced by that global mix because foreign duration demand, reserve behavior, and currency hedging costs all respond to the same relative-rate map.

Fiscal transmission is the common thread across these markets. Japan's long end cheapened on spending plans, Europe judged energy relief through the lens of second-round inflation, and Australia kept the option of further tightening while monitoring housing. The U.S. close therefore sits inside a global duration environment where oil relief helps, but it does not remove the need for labor, wage, fiscal, and supply confirmation.

## EM Sovereigns, Dollar Liquidity, And Debt-Management Risk

EM carried a strong access signal and a clear dispersion warning. The access signal was visible in international issuance. Emerging-market sovereigns and companies sold about 450 billion dollars of dollar- or euro-denominated bonds in the first half of 2026, up 15% from a year earlier and a record for the period. Tight spreads and global demand kept the market open, which matters because higher U.S. real yields usually challenge EM hard-currency funding. The close therefore did not show a simple shutoff in EM financing.

Local-market flows also looked better in selected countries. Indonesia was on pace for the largest monthly foreign bond inflow in 13 months, with foreign funds purchasing a net 1.2 billion dollars of government bonds through June 26. Policy makers had lifted yields and worked to stabilize the rupiah, and the flow response suggests that compensation attracted overseas demand. India showed a broader recovery, with local equities outperforming EM peers, the rupee rebounding, and foreign demand for bonds rising. Foreign investors purchased a record 4.2 billion dollars of Indian bonds in June, helped by tax and ownership reforms and easier access to the sovereign market.

Kenya added a sovereign financing example. The World Bank approved a 750 million dollar loan to support reforms in governance and public-finance management, and it agreed to help Kenya issue a 500 million dollar sustainability-linked loan tied to reforestation and energy access. That support eases debt pressure and can improve the country's ability to manage maturities, but it is still reform-linked and conditional. The issue is public balance-sheet repair and multilateral backing, so it affects sovereign debt management and country funding credibility.

Inflation credibility remained a constraint in several EM markets. Goldman and Oxford Economics expected South Africa to raise rates after two-year inflation expectations rose to 3.9% from 3.6%, above the central bank's 3% target. Colombia lifted its key rate by 75 bp to 12% after a court restored central bank powers, citing above-target inflation expectations and a tight labor market. The country's bonds and currency rallied after the decision, showing that in parts of EM a hike can support fixed-income assets when it restores policy credibility. Chile was a different case, with short-end debt supported by falling inflation expectations and expectations for lower peso yields in July.

Restructuring risk sat beside those stronger stories. Venezuela's defaulted sovereign and PDVSA bonds lost more than 10% since the restructuring process began, as investors priced a bleaker outlook after earthquakes added to existing debt challenges. Ethiopia bondholders criticized the IMF's handling of its debt rework. China discouraged higher-yield offshore borrowing by municipal-linked borrowers, which is also an EM dollar-debt issue when viewed through offshore funding access. These items did not drive the June 30 U.S. rates close, but they matter for the EM credit map because they show where tight global spreads can coexist with country-specific stress.



Market infrastructure also supported selected EM access. European authorities recognized India's sovereign debt clearinghouse as an accepted counterparty, making it easier for banks such as Deutsche Bank and BNP Paribas to trade Indian bonds and derivatives. That detail matters because index inclusion and foreign demand need settlement confidence as much as yield pickup. It adds to the June inflow story without turning India into a one-day U.S. rates driver. The fixed-income read is that operational access, tax treatment, and ownership rules can turn a country into a more durable destination for foreign capital when global yields are volatile.

China's offshore-debt curbs fit the same debt-management frame. The National Development and Reform Commission discouraging higher-yield offshore borrowing by municipal-linked firms reduces the chance that weak borrowers lock in expensive external funding, but it also signals that refinancing risk is being managed through administrative control. That is different from a spread-driven market close. It is a forward constraint on supply, maturity extension, and investor confidence in local-government-linked credit. Combined with Venezuela and Ethiopia restructuring headlines, it keeps the EM read centered on balance-sheet quality, maturity management, and the headline issuance total together.

DXY kept the channel sensitive. The dollar index rose 0.13% to 101.24 on the day and was up 2.36% on the month. EMB fell 0.39%. Those moves leave record issuance and strong Indonesia and India flows intact while making the next leg dependent on U.S. labor and real yields. If payrolls keep the Fed path firm and DXY holds higher, EM access will stay more selective by country, funding purpose, and reform credibility. If labor cools and the dollar softens, the first-half issuance window has room to continue.

## Cross asset

Cross-asset markets accepted the rates selloff more comfortably than credit did. The S&P 500 rose 0.79%, the Nasdaq 100 gained 1.68%, and the semiconductor index climbed 3.92%. VIX fell 6.80% to 16.45. That combination says the equity market was willing to absorb higher Treasury yields, especially through technology and AI-linked growth. The quarterly chip strength also fits the broader story around AI capital expenditure, data-center buildouts, and the financing needed to support them.

The equity signal does not remove the fixed-income tension. AI leverage was already on the radar after IMF officials highlighted maturity mismatch between long-lived physical assets and the debt used to fund them. South Korea and Taiwan AI buildout stories, chip equity gains, and the U.S. wage-watch discussion around construction and skilled trades all connect the equity boom to rates through funding demand and labor costs. A strong SOX tape can improve risk appetite while also keeping capex, wages, and debt issuance in the fixed-income conversation.

Oil gave the rates market a cushion. WTI closed at 70.10, down on the day and almost 20% lower on the month, while Brent was near 73.46. Lower crude helped explain why breakevens rose only 2 bp even as nominal yields and real yields moved higher. Energy traders were still reassessing supply chains and diplomacy, and ECB speakers continued to discuss the pass-through of energy shocks, so oil did not disappear from the inflation story. It restrained inflation compensation while real yields remained the main reason nominal yields rose.

The dollar narrowed the risk read. DXY closed at 101.24, up 0.13% on the day and higher on the month. HSBC had warned that the dollar rally could become explosive if the Fed tightened further, and it also flagged the risk of a Treasury-market reversal after investors had expected curve steepening early in the year. The June 30 close fits the currency part of that warning: a firmer dollar alongside higher real yields tests EM funding and global risk appetite even when U.S. equities are strong.



The yen added another cross-asset channel. Japan's currency was near levels that had previously triggered official intervention, and the weak-yen story is inseparable from Japanese rates. A weak yen can import inflation pressure and pressure the BOJ, while higher Japanese long-end yields create a competing return for domestic investors that historically supported foreign duration. The June 30 U.S. close needed no yen shock to explain Treasury yields. The currency still adds to the global duration map because reserve behavior, intervention risk, and hedged-yield comparisons all matter when U.S. real yields are moving higher.

Gold did little, and credit ETFs were weaker. Gold was almost unchanged around 4,021.8, while LQD and EMB fell. HYG and JNK were only slightly lower, but the investment-grade and EM debt gauges showed that the fixed-income risk board was less enthusiastic than equities. The message from cross asset is therefore balanced: equity breadth and lower volatility joined the higher-yield close by showing risk tolerance, oil restrained inflation compensation, and the dollar plus credit gauges kept the close from becoming a broad easing or spread-compression story.

## Forward Market Significance

ADP and payrolls are the immediate rates test. May openings held near 7.6 million, and that was enough to push real yields higher into the June 30 close. ADP on July 1 and payrolls on July 2 decide whether openings are translating into hiring and wages. A softer hiring sequence would let the front end treat the JOLTS reaction as a one-session adjustment. Firm payrolls, stable participation, or wage pressure would keep the 2Y, 5Y, and 10Y real yield exposed to another repricing.



Confidence details also matter after the close. The headline improvement to 91.2 was helped by lower gasoline prices, which is friendly for consumer purchasing power and inflation psychology. The softer labor perceptions inside the survey point in the other direction. Payrolls will decide which part of that survey mattered more for rates: gasoline relief that lowers inflation anxiety, or labor caution that signals slower hiring. A rates rally would need the second channel without a wage offset. A further selloff would need openings, payrolls, and wages to line up on the resilient side.

Treasury supply matters through absorption, not through a generic calendar label. Late-June bill results and the early-July auction schedule matter because the curve is still carrying a front-end policy premium and the long end remains sensitive to fiscal and global duration news. Strong bill and coupon absorption would let the market focus on labor and inflation. Weak demand, higher concessions, or funding pressure would make the 2s10s and 2s30s moves more vulnerable to a supply premium.

Oil and the dollar decide whether the rates move stays real-yield led. WTI around 70 kept the inflation-compensation channel contained despite the nominal selloff. A rebound in crude or renewed Middle East tension would push the 10Y breakeven and ECB reaction-function discussion back into the foreground. A stable or lower oil price would keep the burden on labor and real yields. DXY around 101 is the parallel EM test: dollar strength would pressure hard-currency borrowers and local-rate defense, while dollar softness would support the first-half EM issuance window.

Credit and primary markets need borrower-level confirmation. Japanese banks and corporates showed that high-quality foreign-currency issuance can clear in size, while China, Dish, private credit, and AI leverage all point to tighter filters for weaker or more levered borrowers. The next issuance sessions matter more than listed credit gauges alone. Full order books, contained concessions, and successful refinancing for mid-quality names would broaden the credit read. Wider concessions, delayed deals, or more restructuring headlines would keep credit behind the equity rally.

Global duration remains live through Japan and Europe. Japan's super-long yields, Meiji Yasuda demand, the two-year JGB auction, and the yen are the clearest signals for whether fiscal pressure is being absorbed by domestic buyers. In Europe, French and German inflation relief must be weighed against the ECB's concern about second-round effects. If oil stays lower and services inflation cools, global duration can support the U.S. rally again. If Japan's long end keeps cheapening or ECB officials lean harder into stagflation risk, U.S. long-end relief will be harder to sustain.

EM follows the same sequence: labor, dollar, real yields, and country reform. Indonesia and India flows, Kenya's World Bank support, and record first-half EM issuance show that access is open. South Africa, Colombia, Venezuela, Ethiopia, and China's offshore debt curbs show that access is not uniform. The next tradable EM signal is whether a stronger U.S. labor print lifts DXY and real yields enough to tighten the funding window, or whether softer hiring lets the strong issuance channel extend into July.

section_summaries.summary: June 30 paired higher U.S. yields with stronger equities and lower volatility, leaving a real-yield-led selloff that risk assets could absorb. The opening read integrates credit, Japan, Europe, EM and cross-asset signals to show why the close was tradable, selective and still dependent on labor confirmation.
section_summaries.market_read: Rates cheapened through real yields while risk assets accepted the move.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: Sets the full FI frame beyond the Treasury tape.
section_summaries.next_check: ADP, payrolls, DXY, oil and primary-market absorption.

section_summaries.section_title: UST Rates, Fed Pricing, Real Yields, And Curve Structure
section_summaries.summary: Treasuries sold off after May JOLTS openings stayed near 7.6 million and confidence improved modestly. The 10Y real yield rose more than breakevens, so the rates move leaned on Fed-path restraint and labor resilience while lower oil limited the inflation-compensation impulse before payrolls. This leaves payrolls central.
section_summaries.market_read: Real yields drove the Treasury selloff more than breakevens.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: Explains the policy and curve channel behind the close.

section_summaries.summary: Credit stayed open for stronger borrowers, led by heavy Japanese overseas issuance and continued bank access, while China offshore-debt curbs, Dish bankruptcy and private-credit stress kept borrower tiering visible. Listed credit lagged equities, so primary absorption and refinancing terms remain the key confirmation points. Watch weaker names closely.
section_summaries.market_read: Primary access held, but borrower quality still controlled funding terms.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: Separates true financing access from equity-led risk appetite.
section_summaries.next_check: New-issue concessions, bank issuance and stressed refinancing headlines.

section_summaries.section_title: Global Duration, Central Banks, And Fiscal Transmission
section_summaries.summary: Japan kept global duration under pressure as super-long yields rose on fiscal concerns, even as domestic liability demand appeared at higher yields. Europe received inflation relief from oil and softer French and German prints, but ECB speakers kept second-round effects and policy optionality alive. Energy remains the hinge.
section_summaries.market_read: Global duration improved only where oil relief met credible absorption.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: Links U.S. long-end risk to Japan, ECB and energy pass-through.
section_summaries.next_check: JGB super-long yields, ECB rhetoric, oil and euro-area inflation.

section_summaries.section_title: EM Sovereigns, Dollar Liquidity, And Debt-Management Risk
section_summaries.summary: EM access remained open through record first-half issuance, Indonesia inflows, India bond demand and Kenya support. The dollar, U.S. real yields, South Africa and Colombia policy risk, Venezuela restructuring losses and China offshore-debt curbs kept the market divided by country balance sheet and credibility.
section_summaries.market_read: EM funding stayed open but depended on dollar and policy credibility.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: Shows where U.S. real yields transmit into sovereign funding.
section_summaries.next_check: DXY, EMB, South Africa, Colombia and new hard-currency issuance.

section_summaries.summary: Equities, semiconductors and volatility supported risk appetite, while oil helped restrain inflation compensation. The firmer dollar and weaker listed FI gauges limited the message, making cross-asset confirmation strongest in equities and weakest in credit and EM debt as payrolls approach. That split matters now. For payrolls.
section_summaries.market_read: Risk appetite improved, but dollar and FI gauges narrowed confirmation.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: Tests whether higher yields are absorbed or become stress.
section_summaries.next_check: SOX, VIX, WTI, DXY, LQD and EMB.

section_summaries.summary: The next market tests are labor translation from openings into hiring, supply absorption, oil and dollar direction, and whether primary credit demand reaches weaker borrowers. Japan, ECB communication and EM dollar liquidity decide whether the U.S. real-yield selloff becomes global pressure or fades. Labor remains the hinge.
section_summaries.market_read: Payrolls, supply, oil, DXY and credit absorption decide persistence.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: Turns the close into concrete next-session market triggers.
section_summaries.next_check: ADP, payrolls, auctions, DXY, oil and issuance concessions.

methodology_note: Synthesized market analysis from market levels and reporting context. Forward Market Significance carries the next market checks.
